(59 comments)

I didn't flag every style issue. Please check it over before you resubmit.

 if (ptr) => if (ptr != NULL)
 if (!ptr) => if (ptr == NULL)
 if ((a == b) && (c == d)) => if (a == b && c == d)
 p = calloc(sizeof(char), sizeof(struct s)) => p = calloc(1, sizeof(*p))
 strncmp => strcmp

Add const to almost every char * input parameter.

Run checkpatch.

Error messages are not capitalized.

Please use struct list_head rather than rolling your own lists.

Patch Set 62:

I find it problematic that this patch was updated on 2014-10-07 but it was based on a commit dated 2014-09-18 (i.e. a three weeks older version of master).  That exposes the patch to three weeks of potentially conflicting commits.

While there is always going to be some delta between the time a patch is submitted and when it lands, it makes sense to minimize this delta as much as possible.

Patch Set 62:

It applies cleanly. I have been using it with the latest master.

Patch Set 62:

James, thanks for the info.

My question isn't so much whether the current patch applies cleanly to master, but why a patch was submitted with a 3-week deficit right from the start that could cause the patch to have a conflict.  Conflicts can either be simple (e.g. patch doesn't apply) or tricky (patch applies, but doesn't do the right thing because some other piece of code has changed in the meantime.

We now have HPDD Checkpatch on the lookout for patches that no longer apply to master, which helps in the first case, but not in the second.

Patch Set 62:

To be clear, this isn't a problem with this specific patch (i.e. don't go refreshing it just because of my comments), but a general comment on the development process being followed.

Patch Set 62:

The patch has been in the system for a 2 weeks.  What's the process in this case?  Should I refresh the patch every once in a while.  The issue is that it takes a while for the tests to pass, so once they pass I don't like touching them again to avoid having to wast time running them through the test system again, especially that there hasn't been any changes.

I think the issue is that it takes a really long time from the point a patch gets the +2 to the point it lands.  This delay opens the window for conflicts due to other patches landing in the mean time.

Patch Set 62:

Amir, my comment wasn't about how long the patch takes from the time it is submitted until it is tested/inspected/landed, but rather that when the patch was most recently submitted on 2014-10-07 that it was based on a master tree that hadn't been updated since 2014-09-18 (parent commit
9924ee329a87cf971ec2d32a64c65fd04bad6022"LU-5396 mdc: add sparse annotation __user wherever needed"). 

Since we already know that the landing process can unfortunately take a long time, it makes sense to base it on a recently updated tree to ensure it is least likely to have conflicts with changes that have recently landed. 

I don't want to make a huge deal about this, but I thought it is something I should point out.

commit.subject: LU-2456 lnet: DLC user space Configuration library
commit.message:
LU-2456 lnet: DLC user space Configuration library

This is the seventh patch of a set of patches that enables DLC.

This patch adds a user space library liblnetconfig, which provides
an API to add/delete/show LNET parameters.  This library has been
added to the /lnet/utils/lnetconfig directory.

There are two sets of APIs which are presented.  There are the
APIs which configure a specific parameter such as routes, and
there are a set of APIs which take in a YAML block of entities
which are then added/deleted/showed.  The latter is intended for
use by IML or other similar tools.

All APIs return a cYAML error block, and the show APIs return a
cYAML show block as well.  The cYAML block is a structural
represenation of a YAML block.  This can be stored/queried/printed
and deleted by the caller of the API.  A cYAML API is provided
to facilitate these operations

DLC can be enabled via --enable-dlc or disabled via --disable-dlc
parameters passed to configure.  DLC is by default enabled.
If the yaml library is installed and --enable-dlc is set then
dlc is added to the build, otherwise if libyaml is not installed
or --disable-dlc is set then dlc is not added to the build.

When building the rpm, this configure options are used to decide
to add the Require and BuildRequire conditions or not.

Currently SLES doesn't include libyaml package so it will need
to be installed manually if users want to build DLC
